title: Las Vegas and a McDonald's Convention
subtitle: Sheltering from the sun, hedonism, and emptiness at McDonald's Worldwide 2026
contentHtml:
In my sixty years of traveling, spanning three different careers and many conventions, I’d never been to Las Vegas. That was mostly intentional, because if given a choice between something hyped as premium, and anything lesser, I’ll choose the lesser. So I’ve been to Reno, Hot Springs, AR, and a variety of other despondent casinos in despondent towns, where the musical act is a band milking a name forged from a single hit decades before — coming this spring to the Zia Park Casino Hotel and Racetrack: Men Without Hats!

Las Vegas is hyperbole manifest, a city built on the promise of excess in the middle of inhospitable desert, as if that promise can ward off the bone-dry blistering sun, turning the desiccated lush. Well, that promise and plenty of AC, windowless labyrinthine casinos, and car services that whisk you from A to B so that you rarely touch the earth. We’ve defeated nature, and Las Vegas is our bounty, our displayed heads of slain warriors, a trophy proclaiming victory.

But now I’d finally come to Vegas for the only thing that could have brought me in June — the McDonald’s worldwide convention, a biennial festival of franchises, management, and vendors that I’d been invited to speak at. My original plan was to attend, then stay for three more days and walk beyond the strip. That evaporated within an hour after landing, as I shuffled from bus stop to bus stop under the 103 degree heat-lamp sun, trying to figure out how to get to my hotel without using a cab. I’d assumed that would be easy, given the thousands who do that daily, but no, apparently nothing in Vegas is easy unless you throw money at it. So after a sweltering shuttle van, and two musty buses, I’d made it to my hotel, desiccated and overheated, realizing you can’t walk Vegas, not in June at least, unless you’re mad, which plenty of people around me were. Ranting, swaddled in rags, twitching from drugs, mad.

I also realized I’d bought into an image of the Strip that was more hype than reality. By the time I reached my hotel I’d resigned myself to being confined to it, figuring I could still happily walk up and down it, the superior vena cava of America’s hedonistic body, gawking at the marvelous. That was also quickly dispelled, after a mile trudging on its shadeless sidewalks lined with fake grass and music pumped in from far-away casinos.

Vegas is spread out, all of it, even the Strip, and going from one casino to the next is akin to passing from one castle to another, through gates manned by bored security guards, over moats of chlorinated water, and around gardens of perfunctory hedges and Uber drivers. The magnificent was there, all lined up in a four-mile avenue, but intentionally fortified, not to keep people out, but to keep them in. The banal was also there, with strip malls of tattoo parlors, fast-food franchises, and vape shops filling in the gaps, only differing from the rest of America in all being open 24 hours, because the city that never sleeps is earned hype.

I wasn’t going to get anything out of walking Vegas this trip, in this heat, other than scorn, so I shelved that for another time, and pivoted to focusing entirely on the convention, which given how much of the last two decades of my life has been built around McDonald’s was fitting.

My life has been tethered to McDonald’s since I left banking and began to write about America, awkwardly at first, but now firmly, and without the least bit of remorse.

McDonald’s is … central to who I am. My first viral piece, that effectively launched my writing career, was about how McDonald’s has become America’s community centers, especially for communities where so much else has fallen apart, or disappointed people. Over half of my book Dignity takes place in McDonald’s, and while writing it, in a McDonald’s, my working title was, “Everything you want to know about America can be learned in a McDonald’s” because I sincerely believe that. Which is why, when I do a brief turn as a political reporter, I spend my months interviewing people in McDonald’s, not at campaign events, or non-profits, or even in diners, because all of those are self-selecting for highly engaged voters and elections are not determined by only the highly engaged voters but by the far larger “normies” who only tune in and turn out every few years.

I now love McDonald's, as a place to be a detached cultural observer, but also as a regular.

Each morning, if possible, I began it in a McDonald's, and there was one adjacent to my hotel, or about as adjacent as anything in Vegas can get, which meant a few football fields’ walk from my hotel compound’s borders. It is where I met the teenage Maria, only a few years in America from Venezuela, her hat blossoming with flair, her mind with the American Dream, which she was sure McDonald's was her first step toward, "I'll work any shift they give me, no matter what hour. That's how it is done, right?"
